Write individual .md files for each table with structure + sample data understanding

<role_definition>
You are Bob, a highly skilled software engineer with extensive knowledge in many programming languages, frameworks, design patterns, and best practices.
</role_definition>

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Investigate and read relevant files before answering questions about the codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless you are certain of the correct answer — give grounded and hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>

<engineering_discipline>
Produce the minimal change that solves the problem. Do not add features, refactors, or abstractions beyond what was asked. Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot happen. Do not clean up surrounding code that is unrelated to the task. If you write something and a simpler version would work, use the simpler version. Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
</engineering_discipline>

<tool_use>
- Choose the most appropriate tool for each step.
- Tools can be called in parallel when independent, but avoid running operations together that have dependencies (e.g., don't run edits and commands that depend on those edits in the same batch).
- If a tool fails, address the error before continuing.
- MCP server tools use IDs formatted as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Treat these as Model Context Protocol tools; the middle segment identifies the owning MCP server. Both segments may be truncated if the full name exceeds the 64-character API limit. Built-in and source tools use their normal IDs and are categorized by their descriptions and capability groups.
- Prefer native file tools (read_file, write_file, search_and_replace, insert_content, list_files) over shell commands for file operations — they need no additional user permissions. Use shell/execute tools only for operations that genuinely require them: builds, test runners, git commands, and system operations.
</tool_use>

<markdown_rules>
All responses should show any language construct or filename reference as a clickable link, exactly as [`filename OR language.declaration()`](relative/file/path.ext:line); line is required for syntax references and optional for filename links.
</markdown_rules>

<auto_appended_context>
Each user message may contain an auto-appended <environment_details> section that includes potentially relevant information such as git status, active file, and relevant files modified externally since your last interaction. This section is automatically appended by the system to provide additional context that may possibly help you and is not part of the user's query.

Pay close attention to the <external_changes> sub-section — it lists files that were modified, deleted, or created outside of your actions. Before writing or editing any file, check whether it appears in <external_changes> and incorporate those changes to avoid conflicts.
</auto_appended_context>

<base_rules>
- Prefer editing tools (apply_diff, search_and_replace, insert_content) over write_file for existing files.
- When using write_file, provide the COMPLETE file content. Partial updates or placeholders are not acceptable.
- Be direct and technical. Do not start messages with "Great", "Certainly", "Okay", or "Sure".
- Follow existing code style and project conventions.
- Use available tools to accomplish tasks efficiently. Do not ask unnecessary follow-up questions.
- Do not stop when implementation looks complete. Finish the task only when all requested requirements are met and the resulting state is positive.
- Before reporting final completion, run the relevant validation for your changes. Use the checks that apply to the work you changed, such as lint, tests, typecheck, formatting, builds, or other project validation.
- Work is done when the changed work and any affected project state pass the required validation with no new warnings, failures, or unfinished requirements.
</base_rules>

You have access to skills that provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. Review the available skills below. When a user's request matches a skill's description, activate it using the use_skill tool to load detailed instructions before proceeding. Only activate skills that are clearly relevant to the current task. Activate each skill once per current context window. If context was compacted and you need the instructions again, you may re-activate that skill. Only call use_skill for skills listed here — do not attempt to invoke skills not present in this list.
<available_skills>
  <skill>
    <name>create-skill</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new Bob skill (SKILL.md) — covers the frontmatter schema, name rules, file location, and gotchas. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a skill.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>create-mode</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new custom Bob mode (custom_modes.yaml) — covers the schema, permission groups, roleDefinition, and file location. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a mode or persona.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>configure-mcp</name>
    <description>Add a new MCP server or diagnose an existing one — covers local vs remote transport, system dependency checks (node, docker, podman, python, uv), config schema validation, secrets handling, and scope. Use when the user wants to add, register, configure, or troubleshoot an MCP server.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>build-mcp-server</name>
    <description>Guide for building a custom MCP server from scratch — covers project scaffolding, the v2 registerTool/registerResource/registerPrompt API, API key and OAuth authentication patterns, building and registering the server. Use when the user wants to create, build, or write a custom MCP server.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>xlsx-insights</name>
    <description>Use this skill when the user asks for analysis, extraction, or insights from an Excel (.xlsx) workbook. It samples the file, generates a small Node script to compute the answer, runs it, and summarises the result.</description>
  </skill>
</available_skills>

<user_custom_instructions>

Language Preference:
You should always speak and think in the "English" (en) language unless the user gives you instructions below to do otherwise.

You can get information about available symbols by using the `GetSymbolsOverview` tool for finding top-level symbols in a file,
or by using `FindSymbol` if you already know the symbol's name path. You generally try to read as little code as possible
while still solving your task, meaning you only read the bodies when you need to, and after you have found the symbol you want to edit.
For example, if you are working with python code and already know that you need to read the body of the constructor of the class Foo, you can directly
use `FindSymbol` with the name path `Foo/__init__` and `include_body: true`. If you don't know yet which methods in `Foo` you need to read or edit,
you can use `FindSymbol` with the name path `Foo`, `include_body: false` and `depth: 1` to get all (top-level) methods of `Foo` before proceeding
to read the desired methods with `include_body: true`
You can understand relationships between symbols by using the `FindReferencingSymbols` tool.

# create_html_artifact — one-pager output
Use ONLY when an HTML one-pager makes information substantially more consumable than markdown — reports, tables, dashboards. NEVER for short answers or content that reads naturally inline.
Tool call must be the LAST action in the turn; keep chat to one or two sentences.
Produce a single self-contained HTML+CSS document. Content is sanitized on save — forbidden patterns are stripped regardless of instructions.
End with a small muted "Made with IBM Bob" footer before `</body>` (centred, ~12px, thin top border).
Target a report, not an interactive app/demo. Event handlers are stripped for security; use file generation for apps.

## Never allowed — always stripped, even if instructed
<script>, inline event handlers (onclick/onload/…), <iframe>/<frame>/<frameset>/<object>/<embed>/<applet>/<portal>, <form>, <link>, <base>, <meta http-equiv>, javascript:/vbscript: URLs, protocol-relative URLs (//…), external URLs in src/poster/… attributes, @import in <style> blocks, url() in <style> blocks unless it is a data: URI.
<a href> to external sites is fine. Inline data: image URIs are fine.

## Design
Single column, max-width ~760px, centred. No animations, gradients, shadows, emojis, or decorative icons. SVG only for data visualisation.
Font: `-apple-system, "Segoe UI", system-ui, sans-serif`, ~14–15px, line-height ~1.6.
Palette: bg `#ffffff`, surface `#f7f8fa`, border `#e5e7eb`, text `#1f2328`, muted `#57606a`, accent `#3b82d4` (sparingly), secondary `#7c5cd8` (rarely).

Re-use the same `id` to publish a new version.

# glob — file search
Use `glob` to find files by name pattern (e.g. "**/*.ts", "src/**/*.test.ts"). Prefer this over `list_files` when looking for specific files across the project.
Only fall back to `list_files` when you need to browse a single directory's structure.

# grep — content search
Use `grep` to find code by content using regex patterns. Prefer this over `list_files` + `read_file` when searching for specific code, symbols, or patterns.
Use `read_file` only after grep tells you which file and lines to look at.

# Subagents
You have access to `spawn_subagent` which creates an independent agent with its own context.

**Default: do the work yourself.** Most tasks are faster and cheaper without a subagent.
Only consider a subagent when ALL of these apply:
- The task is clearly self-contained and only needs a summary back
- It would add significant irrelevant content to your context
- You cannot accomplish it with 1-2 direct tool calls

Available types:
- "explore": Explore codebase (read tools, explorer model)
- "general": Full tools, default model

**fork_context**: Set to true when the subagent needs to understand prior conversation — e.g., decisions made, constraints discovered, or user preferences expressed earlier.
Leave false (default) when the description alone is sufficient context.

Do NOT use subagents for:
- Simple file reads, searches, or single-tool operations
- Tasks where you already have relevant context
- Quick lookups that would take fewer turns done directly

data.content:
# Build a Custom MCP Server

Guide the user through designing and building a new MCP server from scratch — a Node.js process
(or remote HTTP endpoint) that exposes tools, resources, and/or prompts to Bob. Follow these steps
in order.

## Step 0 — Decide Whether a Custom Server Is the Right Fit

Push back if it isn't. A custom server is warranted when:

- The user needs a **reusable, stateful integration** with an external API or data source across
  many tasks.
- The required capability involves **multi-step protocol negotiation** (OAuth token exchange,
  streaming data, pagination) that is too brittle to do ad-hoc with `execute_command`.
- The user explicitly says "create an MCP server" or "add a tool that…".

A custom server is **not** needed when:
- A one-off shell script or `execute_command` call solves the problem.
- An existing MCP server in the marketplace already covers the use-case.
- The task is a single API call with no reuse value.

## Step 1 — Gather Requirements

Use `ask_followup_question` to establish:

- **What capability does the server expose?** (tools, prompts, or a mix — see note below on resources)
- **What external API/service does it integrate with?** Does it require an API key or OAuth?
- **Transport:** local stdio (spawned by Bob as a child process) or remote HTTP (a running service)?
  Default to **local stdio** unless the user has a specific reason for HTTP.
- **Language:** default to **TypeScript/Node.js** unless the user prefers Python or another runtime.
- **Scope:** should it be registered globally or workspace-only in `mcp.json`?

## Step 2 — Scaffold the Project

### TypeScript / Node.js (default)

Create the project directory and install dependencies:

```bash
mkdir <server-name>
cd <server-name>
npm init -y
npm install @modelcontextprotocol/server zod
npm install -D @types/node typescript
mkdir src && touch src/index.ts
```

Set `package.json` fields:

```json
{
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": { "build": "tsc && chmod 755 build/index.js" },
  "bin": { "<server-name>": "./build/index.js" },
  "files": ["build"]
}
```

Use this `tsconfig.json`:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "Node16",
    "moduleResolution": "Node16",
    "outDir": "./build",
    "rootDir": "./src",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
```

## Step 3 — Implement the Server

### Minimal stdio server skeleton (v2 API)

```typescript
#!/usr/bin/env node
import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/server";
import { StdioServerTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/server/stdio";
import { z } from "zod/v4";

const server = new McpServer({ name: "<server-name>", version: "0.1.0" });

// Register tools and prompts here (see below)

async function main() {
  const transport = new StdioServerTransport();
  await server.connect(transport);
  console.error("<server-name> running on stdio");
}

main().catch((error) => {
  console.error("Fatal error:", error);
  process.exit(1);
});
```

⚠️ **Always use `console.error` for logging** — `console.log` writes to stdout, which is the MCP
protocol channel; any non-JSON output there will corrupt the connection.

### Registering a tool (v2 API)

Use `server.registerTool`. Always wrap input schemas in `z.object()`. Return `isError: true` for
recoverable failures so Bob can self-correct:

```typescript
server.registerTool(
  "get-weather",
  {
    description: "Get current weather for a city",
    inputSchema: z.object({
      city: z.string().describe("City name"),
    }),
  },
  async ({ city }) => {
    try {
      const data = await fetchWeather(city);
      return { content: [{ type: "text", text: JSON.stringify(data) }] };
    } catch (error) {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text", text: `Failed: ${error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)}` }],
        isError: true,
      };
    }
  }
);
```

> Bob discovers MCP resources and resource templates, and can read resources by URI. Use resources for
> contextual data; use tools for actions.

### Registering a prompt

```typescript
server.registerPrompt(
  "summarize",
  { argsSchema: z.object({ text: z.string() }) },
  async ({ text }) => ({
    messages: [{ role: "user", content: { type: "text", text: `Summarize: ${text}` } }],
  })
);
```

## Step 4 — Handle Authentication

### API key (simplest)

Read credentials from environment variables — never hardcode them:

```typescript
const API_KEY = process.env.MY_API_KEY;
if (!API_KEY) throw new Error("MY_API_KEY environment variable is required");
```

Bob/the user injects these via the `env` block in `mcp.json`.

⚠️ Bob **cannot** expand `${VAR}` references in `mcp.json` — values are stored literally. Walk
the user through obtaining the key, then use `ask_followup_question` to collect it before writing
the config.

### OAuth (requires a one-time setup script)

MCP servers run non-interactively — they cannot open browser windows or initiate OAuth flows at
runtime. The pattern is:

1. Write a **separate one-time script** (e.g. `get-refresh-token.js`) that:
   - Starts a local HTTP server to receive the OAuth callback
   - Opens the authorization URL (or prints it for the user to visit)
   - Logs the resulting refresh token to stderr/stdout
2. Instruct the user to run the script once with `execute_command` to capture the token.
3. Put the refresh token in the server's `env` block in `mcp.json`.
4. The server exchanges the refresh token for access tokens at runtime.

## Step 5 — Build

```bash
npm run build
```

Verify the output file exists at `build/index.js` before registering it.

## Step 6 — Register in mcp.json

After a successful build, register the server using the `configure-mcp` skill (Path A). Key
points:

- Set `command` to `"node"` and `args` to the **absolute path** of `build/index.js`.
- Add all required env vars to the `env` block.
- Default `disabled` to `false` (omit it) and `alwaysAllow` to `[]` (omit it).

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "<server-name>": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["/absolute/path/to/<server-name>/build/index.js"],
      "env": {
        "MY_API_KEY": "<user-provided>"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Step 7 — Verify and Demonstrate

Once the config is saved, Bob hot-reloads the server. Confirm it appears as connected in Bob's MCP
panel, then suggest a concrete command the user can try to exercise the new tool — e.g. "You can
now ask: what's the weather in London?"

If the server fails to connect, use the `configure-mcp` skill (Path B) to diagnose.

## Notes for power users

- **HTTP transport:** for a remotely hosted server, use `NodeStreamableHTTPServerTransport` from
  `@modelcontextprotocol/node` and register the server with a `url` instead of `command`/`args`.
- **Editing an existing server:** locate the server's source from its `args` path, edit it with
  `apply_diff`/`write_file`, rebuild, and the server will reconnect automatically.
- **Multiple tools:** keep each tool focused on one operation. Prefer more small tools over one
  large tool with a `mode` parameter.

## Reminders

- Use `ask_followup_question` for API keys and OAuth tokens — never guess or placeholder them.
- Always use `console.error` for server-side logging, never `console.log`.
- Use `registerTool` / `registerResource` / `registerPrompt` (v2 API) — not the old `server.tool()` variadic form.
- Never include information without evidence.

Everything is done. Here's a summary of what was built:

---

### `mssql-mcp-server` — complete

**Location:** [`mssql-mcp-server/src/index.ts`](mssql-mcp-server/src/index.ts)

**3 tools registered:**

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| `list-databases` | Lists all user databases on the instance (excludes system DBs) |
| `list-tables-by-database-name` | Lists all tables + schema in a given database |
| `execute-query` | Runs a raw T-SQL `SELECT` query against a specified database |

**Safety:** `execute-query` blocks any non-`SELECT`/`WITH` statement before it reaches the server.

**Credentials** are read from env vars (`MSSQL_SERVER`, `MSSQL_USER`, `MSSQL_PASSWORD`) — already wired into [`.bob/mcp.json`](.bob/mcp.json).

Bob will hot-reload the server. You can test it immediately by asking: *"list all databases on the SQL Server"*.

data.content: TCP port is NULL — SQL Server Express is using a **named pipe / dynamic port**, not a fixed TCP port. The `mssql` Node.js driver needs special config for this. Let me update the server to use the `instanceName` option instead:
The `mssql` driver needs `server` set to just the hostname and `instanceName` set separately for named instances. Let me fix this:

arguments.diff:
<<<<<<< SEARCH
:start_line:7
-------
// ─── Connection config ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
const baseConfig: sql.config = {

# Agents

## Overview
A dimension table describing call center agents. Each row represents one agent assigned to a specific call center location with a defined manager and team lead hierarchy.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Agent_ID | Business key for the agent |
| Agent | Full name of the agent |
| Manager | Full name of the agent's manager |
| Team Lead | Full name of the team lead |
| Call Center | Name of the call center the agent belongs to |
| Territory | Geographic territory (e.g. North America) |
| Country | Country of the call center |
| City | City of the call center |

## Sample Data
- Agent IDs 1–5 all belong to "Maritime Operations" call center in Halifax, Canada.
- All report to manager "Spencer Brett Franklin" under team lead "Lester Leon Barrett".

## Relationships
- `Agent_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Agent_ID` (fact table for call center metrics)

# Attrition_Study

## Overview
A denormalized analytical table (blended fact/dimension) capturing employee attrition, performance, training, and fleet metrics in a single wide row per employee event. Used for workforce analytics and predictive attrition modelling.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| Employee_ID | Foreign key to Employee |
| Active Headcount | 1 if currently active, 0 if separated |
| # Separation | Number of separations |
| # Involuntary Separation | Count of involuntary separations |
| # Voluntary Separation | Count of voluntary separations |
| Performance | Performance score |
| Tenure | Tenure in months |
| Age at Metric Date | Age in months at measurement date |
| Annual Salary | Annual compensation |
| Job Family / Job / Position | Role classification |
| Geography / Country / Location / Town or City | Location details |
| Gender / Ethnic Group | Demographic attributes |
| Organization / Organization Class / Organization Type | Org hierarchy |
| Training Hours/Cost/Score/Duration/# Course | Training metrics (overall + by type: Technical, Leadership, Sales) |
| Age of Primary Truck | Age of assigned fleet vehicle |
| Miles Driven TTM (k) | Miles driven in trailing twelve months (thousands) |
| $E-# Separation | Predicted probability of separation |

## Sample Data
- Rows cover employees across Europe and North America in Production jobs.
- Many employees have zero training (0 hours, 0 cost).

## Relationships
- `Employee_ID` → `Employee.Employee_ID`
- Overlaps dimensionally with `HR_Fact` and `Workforce_Fact` but is a standalone analytical extract

# Applicant

## Overview
A dimension table for job applicants used in the recruiting process. Classifies applicants by type (Internal/External) and employment basis (Full Time, Part Time, Student).

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Applicant_ID | Business key for the applicant |
| Applicant | Applicant identifier code (e.g. ID1001) |
| Applicant Type | Internal or External |
| Full Part Time | Employment type: Full Time, Part Time, or Student |

## Sample Data
- Mix of Internal/External applicants with Full Time, Part Time, and Student designations.
- Applicant codes follow the pattern ID1001, ID1002, etc.

## Relationships
- `Applicant_ID` → referenced as `Application_ID` in `Recruiting_Fact` (recruiting metrics fact table)

# Call_Center_Fact

## Overview
The primary fact table for call center operations. Each row represents one call interaction event, linking agents, customers, products, call severity, and service type with detailed call performance metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Agent_ID | FK to Agents |
| Date_ID | FK to Time (date dimension) |
| Group_ID | FK to Group (agent group/level) |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Call_Severity_ID | FK to Call_Severity |
| Service_Type_ID | FK to Service_Type |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| # Call | Number of calls |
| # Completed | Completed calls count |
| # Active Agent | Active agents at time of call |
| # Terminated | Terminated calls |
| Avg Hold Time | Average hold time (minutes) |
| Call Duration | Total call duration (minutes) |
| Call Costs | Cost of calls |
| Hold Time Mising SLA / Hold Time with SLA | SLA compliance for hold time |
| Call Duration missing SLA / Call Duration within SLA | SLA compliance for duration |
| Quality Level | Quality score (0–1) |
| Satisfaction Level | Customer satisfaction score (0–1) |
| Tech Level | Technical resolution score (0–1) |

## Sample Data
- Calls span various agents, products, groups, and service types.
- Quality and satisfaction levels typically range 0.7–1.0.

## Relationships
- `Agent_ID` → `Agents.Agent_ID`
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Group_ID` → `Group.Group_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Call_Severity_ID` → `Call_Severity.Call_Severity_ID`
- `Service_Type_ID` → `Service_Type.Service_Type_ID`
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`

# Customer

## Overview
A dimension table for customers. Each row represents a customer account with classification, geographic, credit, and sales representative details.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Customer_ID | Business key for the customer |
| Customer | Customer name |
| Customer Type No / Customer Type | Type classification (e.g. Retail, Internet Direct, Branded Stores, Entertainment Venues) |
| Customer Code | Numeric customer code |
| Credit Rating | High, Medium, or Low |
| Active | Y/N flag |
| Country / State / City / Address | Geographic location |
| Status | Active or Inactive |
| Sales Rep ID / Sales Rep | Assigned sales representative |
| Receivables ID / Receivables Analyst | Accounts receivable contact |

## Sample Data
- Customers span Germany, Italy, Ireland, Poland — international scope.
- Types include Entertainment Venues, Retail, Internet Direct, Branded Stores.

## Relationships
- `Customer_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Customer_Service_Fact.Customer_ID`, `CustomerLoyalty.Customer_ID`, `Finance_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Order_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Pipeline_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Procurement_Fact` (indirect)

# Call_Severity

## Overview
A small lookup/dimension table defining severity levels for customer support calls. Contains only 3 rows.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Call_Severity_ID | Business key for the severity level |
| Call_Severity | Label: High, Medium, or Low |

## Sample Data
| Call_Severity_ID | Call_Severity |
|---|---|
| 1 | High |
| 2 | Medium |
| 3 | Low |

## Relationships
- `Call_Severity_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Call_Severity_ID`

# Course

## Overview
A dimension table describing training courses available to employees. Categorises courses by type, delivery method, developmental program, and whether they are internal or external.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Course_ID | Business key for the course |
| Course | Course name (e.g. "Advanced Sales Training") |
| Course Type | Category: Sales Skill, Technical Skill, Leadership |
| Delivery Method | How delivered (e.g. Classroom) |
| Developmental Program | Program grouping (e.g. "Sales Core") |
| Course Location | Where held: Head Office or Field Office |
| Internal/External Course | Whether run internally or by an external provider |

## Sample Data
- Courses include sales, technical, and leadership training.
- All sample rows use Classroom delivery.

## Relationships
- `Course_ID` → referenced by `Training_Fact.Course_ID`

# Customer_Service_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for customer service orders/transactions. Tracks order revenue, returns, late products, and customer gain/loss metrics over time per product and customer.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| Sales_ID | FK to Geography (sales rep territory) |
| Revenue | Actual revenue |
| Return Revenue | Revenue lost to returns |
| # Lost Customer / # New Customer | Customer churn and acquisition count |
| Lost Customer % / New Customer % | Churn/acquisition rates |
| # Late Product / Late Product Days | Late delivery metrics |
| Return to Order % | Return rate vs orders |
| Units Ordered / Units Returned | Volume metrics |
| Order Amt | Order amount |

## Sample Data
- Revenue ranges from $56 to $1,008 per row.
- Units ordered 4–9 with small fractional returns.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Sales_ID` → `Geography.Sales_ID`

# Channel

## Overview
A small lookup/dimension table for sales channels. Contains 3 rows representing the available distribution channels.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Channel_ID | Business key for the channel |
| Channel | Channel name: Direct, Retail, or Internet |

## Sample Data
| Channel_ID | Channel |
|---|---|
| 1 | Direct |
| 2 | Retail |
| 3 | Internet |

## Relationships
- `Channel_ID` → referenced by `CustomerLoyalty.Channel_ID`, `Order_Fact.Channel_ID`, `Pipeline_Fact` (implicit via Sales)

# Finance_Fact

## Overview
The primary financial fact table. Each row captures a financial period snapshot per customer, organization, and vendor — including P&L, balance sheet ratios, payables/receivables, and cash flow metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| Date_ID | FK to Fiscal_Period |
| Org_ID | FK to Organization |
| Vendor_ID | FK to Vendor |
| Total Revenue | Total revenue |
| Cost of Goods Sold | COGS |
| Gross Profit | Revenue minus COGS |
| Operating Expense | OpEx |
| Net Income | Gross Profit minus OpEx |
| Budget / Variance | Budget vs actual |
| Current Assets / Current Liabilities | Balance sheet items |
| Opening Inventory / Closing Inventory | Inventory values |
| Inventory Turnover / Current Ratio / Quick Ratio | Liquidity ratios |
| Invoice Amt / Payment Amt / Late Payment | AR/AP metrics |
| DSO / DPO | Days Sales Outstanding / Days Payable Outstanding |
| # Invoice / # Open Invoice / # Payment | Transaction counts |
| Cash Inflow / Cash Outflow | Cash flow |
| Discounts Offered / Discount Taken / Discount Refused | Discount tracking |

## Sample Data
- Net income ranges from negative (−$18,183) to positive ($35,669) per period.
- DSO ~51–72 days, DPO ~49–68 days.

## Relationships
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Date_ID` → `Fiscal_Period.Date_ID`
- `Org_ID` → `Organization.Org_ID`
- `Vendor_ID` → `Vendor.Vendor_ID`

# Fiscal_Period

## Overview
A fiscal calendar dimension table. Maps `Date_ID` values to fiscal year, quarter, and month labels (using "FY" prefix). Used specifically by financial fact tables.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | Business key for the fiscal period |
| Year | Fiscal year label (e.g. "FY09") |
| Quarter No / Quarter | Quarter number and label (e.g. "Q1/FY09") |
| Month No / Month | Month number and label (e.g. "P01/FY09") |

## Sample Data
- Fiscal year starts at FY09 (2009).
- Months labelled as P01 through P12 within each fiscal year.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → referenced by `Finance_Fact.Date_ID`
- Note: Different from `Time` and `HR_Calendar` — this is the **fiscal** calendar used for finance reporting

# Enrollment_Status

## Overview
A small lookup/dimension table for training course enrollment statuses. Defines all possible states a training enrollment can be in.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Enrollment_ID | Business key for the status |
| Enrollment Status | Status label |

## Sample Data
| Enrollment_ID | Enrollment Status |
|---|---|
| 1 | Completed |
| 2 | Development Course Complete |
| 3 | Dropped |
| 4 | Failed |
| 5 | In Progress |

## Relationships
- `Enrollment_ID` → referenced by `Training_Fact.Enrollment_Status` (stored as numeric ID)

# Fleet_Operations

## Overview
This table returned **0 rows** in the sample query. It is defined in the schema but contains no data (or data is filtered/empty).

## Columns
No sample data available. The table likely tracks fleet/vehicle operational metrics based on its name and the fleet-related columns seen in `Attrition_Study` (Age of Primary Truck, Miles Driven TTM).

## Relationships
- Likely links to `Employee` or `Organization` for vehicle assignments
- May share `Date_ID` with the `Time` dimension

# Employee

## Overview
A dimension table for employees. Each row represents one employee with full demographic, role, organizational, and geographic profile.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Employee_ID | Business key for the employee |
| Employee / Employee Name | Employee name |
| Employee ID | Numeric employee ID |
| Age | Age in years (decimal) |
| Date of Birth | DOB |
| Gender | Male/Female |
| Ethnic Group | Ethnicity |
| Marital Status | Marital status (may be null) |
| Disabled Flag | Disability flag (may be null) |
| Job Family / Job / Position | Role classification |
| Original Hire Date | Date first hired |
| Geography / Country / Location / Town or City | Location details |
| Organization / Organization Class / Organization Type | Org hierarchy |
| Tenure | Tenure band (e.g. "(5) 10+", "(4) 5 to <10") |

## Sample Data
- Employees span France, UK, Canada, USA.
- Job families include Production and Operations.
- Tenure bands range from 5–10+ years.

## Relationships
- `Employee_ID` → referenced by `HR_Fact.Employee_ID`, `Workforce_Fact.Employee_ID`, `Attrition_Study.Employee_ID`, `Recruiting_Fact.Employee_ID`, `Training_Fact.Employee_ID`
- `Employee ID` (numeric) → referenced by `Geography.Employee ID`

# Group

## Overview
A small lookup/dimension table defining call center agent group levels. Categorises agents into sales or technical skill levels (Level 1–3).

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Group_ID | Business key for the group |
| Group | Group label (e.g. "Level 1 - Sales", "Level 2 - Technical") |

## Sample Data
| Group_ID | Group |
|---|---|
| 1 | Level 1 - Sales |
| 2 | Level 1 - Technical |
| 3 | Level 2 - Sales |
| 4 | Level 2 - Technical |
| 5 | Level 3 |

## Relationships
- `Group_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Group_ID`

# Geography

## Overview
A dimension table for sales territories and sales representatives. Maps a `Sales_ID` to a territory, country, province, city, and assigned sales rep with their employee ID.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Sales_ID | Business key for the sales territory/rep |
| Territory | High-level territory name (e.g. Europe) |
| Country | Country |
| Province | Province or region |
| City | City |
| Sales Rep | Name of the sales representative |
| Employee ID | FK to Employee (numeric employee ID) |

## Sample Data
- First 5 rows all belong to the Europe territory (England and Scotland).
- Sales reps include Paul Pierce, Pablo Ruiz, Tom Jones, Allison MacBrath, Gary Trent.

## Relationships
- `Sales_ID` → referenced by `CustomerLoyalty.Sales_ID`, `Customer_Service_Fact.Sales_ID`, `Order_Fact.Sales_ID`, `Pipeline_Fact.Sales_ID`
- `Employee ID` → `Employee.Employee ID`

# HR_Calendar

## Overview
A calendar dimension for HR reporting. Maps `Date_ID` values to calendar year, quarter, and month using standard calendar year (not fiscal). Used specifically by HR fact tables.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | Business key for the period |
| Year | Calendar year (e.g. 2009) |
| Quarter No / Quarter | Quarter number and label (e.g. "Q1/2009") |
| Month No / Month | Month number and name (e.g. "January") |

## Sample Data
- Starts from January 2009.
- Month names are spelled out (January, February...) unlike `Fiscal_Period` which uses "P01/FY09".

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → referenced by `HR_Fact.Date_ID` and `Workforce_Fact.Date_ID`
- Parallel to `Fiscal_Period` (finance) and `Time` (daily operations) — each serves a different subject area

# HR_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for core HR metrics per employee per period. Tracks headcount, salary, separations, promotions, and performance distribution over time.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to HR_Calendar |
| Employee_ID | FK to Employee |
| Active Headcount | 1 if active, 0 if not |
| Headcount | Total headcount count |
| Annual Salary / Avg Salary Amt | Compensation metrics |
| # Involuntary Separation / # Voluntary Separation / # Separation | Separation counts |
| Separation % | Separation rate |
| # Promotion / Promotion % | Promotion metrics |
| # High Performers / # Steady Performers / # Low Performers | Performance buckets |
| High Performers % / Steady Performers % / Low Performers % | Performance distribution ratios |

## Sample Data
- Each row is one employee for one period.
- Salaries range from $41,300 to $142,789.
- Performance is categorised as High, Steady, or Low — one flag set per row.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `HR_Calendar.Date_ID`
- `Employee_ID` → `Employee.Employee_ID`

# IT_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for IT/BI system usage metrics. Tracks user sessions, report executions, server performance, and application usage across the BI infrastructure.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Day_ID | FK to Time (daily grain) |
| Period | Peak or off-peak period |
| Server | Server name (e.g. COGVMLN4) |
| ServerFarm | Server farm type (e.g. AIX) |
| ApplicationType | Type of application (e.g. BI) |
| ServerLocation | Physical location of the server |
| Application | Application name (e.g. "Procurement Analytics") |
| Status | Execution status (Success/Failure) |
| NoofRequests | Number of requests in the session |
| User | Username |
| NoofUsers | Number of users in the session |
| ExecutionTime (Secs) | Execution time in seconds |
| Session duration (Mins) | Session length in minutes |
| CPUUsage | CPU utilisation |
| Failures | Number of failures |

## Sample Data
- All sample rows are "Success" status, off-peak period, AIX server farm.
- Applications include Procurement Analytics, Supply Chain, Financial Reporting, etc.
- CPU usage is low (~0.08–0.18).

## Relationships
- `Day_ID` → `Time.Date_ID` (daily calendar grain)

# Procurement_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for procurement/purchasing operations. Tracks purchase orders per buyer, vendor, product, and organisation with compliance, rejection, and delivery performance metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Buyer_ID | ID of the buyer (employee making the purchase) |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Org_ID | FK to Organization |
| Vendor_ID | FK to Vendor |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Compliance Amt | Amount purchased within contract |
| Purchase Amt | Total purchase amount |
| Purchase Amt Outside Contract | Amount purchased outside contract |
| Potential Saving | Potential cost saving |
| Unit Price | Price per unit |
| Units Purchased / Units Rejected / Units Received | Volume and quality metrics |
| Units Purchased Outside Contract | Non-compliant volume |
| # Order / # Reject | Order and rejection counts |
| # Late Delivery / Late Delivery Days | Delivery performance |
| Rejected to Received % | Rejection rate |
| Received to Ordered % | Fulfilment rate |

## Sample Data
- Fulfilment rates ~80–97%.
- Rejection rates up to 23%.
- Some orders have zero late deliveries.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Org_ID` → `Organization.Org_ID`
- `Vendor_ID` → `Vendor.Vendor_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Buyer_ID` → likely maps to `Employee.Employee_ID`

# Organization

## Overview
A dimension table defining the corporate organizational hierarchy. Maps `Org_ID` to three levels of the organisation tree.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Org_ID | Business key for the organisation unit |
| Organization_Level1 | Top-level (e.g. Corporate) |
| Organization_Level2 | Second level (e.g. Sales and Service, Marketing) |
| Organization_Level3 | Third level (e.g. Worldwide Sales, Services, Business Development) |

## Sample Data
- All rows roll up to "Corporate" at Level 1.
- Level 2 includes "Sales and Service" and "Marketing".
- Level 3 includes Worldwide Sales, Services, Business Development, Demand Generation, Events.

## Relationships
- `Org_ID` → referenced by `Finance_Fact.Org_ID`, `Procurement_Fact.Org_ID`

# CustomerLoyalty

## Overview
A wide fact/analytical table combining product, customer, sales, and channel data with detailed revenue, cost, margin, discount, and RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) loyalty scoring metrics. Used for customer segmentation and churn prediction.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID / Customer_ID | Customer reference |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Sales_ID | FK to Geography |
| Channel_ID | FK to Channel |
| Planned / Revenue / Gross Revenue / Variance / Variance % | Revenue planning vs actuals |
| Costs / Margin / Margin % | Profitability |
| Discount / Discount % | Discount applied |
| Units Ordered / Units Shipped | Volume |
| List Price / Sale Price / Unit Cost | Pricing |
| OrderID | Order reference |
| rfm_Recency / rfm_Frequency / rfm_Monetary | Raw RFM values |
| Recency Score / Frequency Score / Monetary Score / RFM Score | RFM scoring (1–5 scale) |
| Churned | Churn flag (0/1) |
| $N-Churned / $NC-Churned / $RP-Churned | Churn probability scores |
| PossibleRevenueLoss | Estimated revenue at risk from churn |
| Product Category/Type, Customer Type, Credit Rating, etc. | Embedded dimension attributes |

## Sample Data
- All sample rows show Product "Computer Accessories" across different customers.
- RFM scores vary widely (e.g. 122, 455, 155) indicating diverse loyalty segments.
- Churned = 0 for all samples but churn probability varies (0.0002 to 0.46).

## Relationships
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Sales_ID` → `Geography.Sales_ID`
- `Channel_ID` → `Channel.Channel_ID`

# Pipeline_Fact

## Overview
A sales pipeline fact table tracking opportunity progression through sales stages. Each row represents a pipeline snapshot per date, customer, product, sales rep, stage, and lead source with win/loss and revenue metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Sales_ID | FK to Geography |
| Stage_ID | FK to Sales_Stage |
| Lead_ID | FK to Lead |
| # Win Opportunity | Count of won opportunities |
| Opportunity Revenue / Revenue | Potential and actual revenue |
| # Lost Opportunity / # Closed Opportunity / # Total Opportunity | Opportunity counts |
| Win/Loss % | Win rate |
| Avg Win Duration | Average days to win |
| Stage to Stage Duration | Days spent in stage |
| Stage to Win % / Stage to Win Duration | Stage-level win probability and duration |
| Units Ordered | Units associated with opportunity |
| Opportunity Amt | Opportunity value |

## Sample Data
- Win/Loss % ranges ~47–64%.
- Revenue from won opportunities ranges from $6,396 to $98,271.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Sales_ID` → `Geography.Sales_ID`
- `Stage_ID` → `Sales_Stage.Stage_ID`
- `Lead_ID` → `Lead.Lead_ID`

# Lead

## Overview
A small lookup/dimension table for sales lead sources. Categorises how pipeline opportunities were originated — by Marketing or Sales channels.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Lead_ID | Business key for the lead source |
| Lead Org | Organisation responsible: Marketing or Sales |
| Lead | Lead source name (e.g. Trade Show, Campaign, Web, Inside Sales, Direct) |

## Sample Data
- Marketing leads: Trade Show, Campaign, Web
- Sales leads: Inside Sales, Direct, (others not shown)

## Relationships
- `Lead_ID` → referenced by `Pipeline_Fact.Lead_ID`

# Recruiting_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for recruitment activity. Each row represents a hiring event combining an employee (hiring manager), applicant, recruiting source, and date with cost, fill-time, and hire-rate metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Employee_ID | FK to Employee (hiring manager or requisition owner) |
| Application_ID | FK to Applicant |
| Recruiting_ID | FK to Recruiting |
| # Applicant Hire | Number of hires (fractional) |
| # Applicants | Total applicants for the role |
| # Open Requisition | Open positions |
| Applicant Hire % | Hire rate |
| Cost per Hire | Recruiting cost per hire |
| Time to Fill | Days taken to fill the position |

## Sample Data
- Cost per hire ranges from $329 to $25,975.
- Time to fill ranges from 16 to 99 days.
- Hire rates vary from 32% to 91%.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Employee_ID` → `Employee.Employee_ID`
- `Application_ID` → `Applicant.Applicant_ID`
- `Recruiting_ID` → `Recruiting.Recruiting_ID`

# Recruiting

## Overview
A dimension table for recruiting channels and sources. Classifies where job candidates come from (agency, employee referral, etc.) and the specific source within that channel.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Recruiting_ID | Business key for the recruiting source |
| Recruiting_Channel | High-level channel (e.g. Agency, Employee Referral) |
| Recruiting_Source | Specific source name (e.g. "Workforce Professionals", "Refer a Friend Program") |

## Sample Data
- Three agency sources: Workforce Professionals, Job Resources, Last Chance Jobs, The Job Experts.
- One employee referral source: Refer a Friend Program.

## Relationships
- `Recruiting_ID` → referenced by `Recruiting_Fact.Recruiting_ID`

# ReturnsForecast

## Overview
A time-series forecast table for product returns. Each row represents a monthly aggregate of actual return metrics alongside time-series model forecasts and confidence intervals. Used for predictive analytics and returns planning.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| Year / Month No | Calendar period of the actuals |
| Record_Count | Number of underlying transactions |
| Return Revenue_Sum / Return Revenue_Mean | Actual return revenue (total and average) |
| Units Returned_Sum / Units Returned_Mean | Actual units returned (total and average) |
| $TI_TimeIndex / $TI_TimeLabel / $TI_Year / $TI_Month | Time series index and label |
| $TI_Future | Flag: 0 = historical, 1 = future forecast |
| $TS-Return Revenue_Sum / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + lower/upper confidence interval for Revenue Sum |
| $TS-Return Revenue_Mean / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + CI for Revenue Mean |
| $TS-Units Returned_Sum / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + CI for Units Returned Sum |
| $TS-Units Returned_Mean / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + CI for Units Returned Mean |

## Sample Data
- Actuals from 2014; time-series projections mapped to 2016.
- Return revenue sums ~$2,339–$3,715 per month.
- Confidence intervals span ~±$1,000.

## Relationships
- Standalone forecast table; not directly joined to other tables via foreign keys
- Logically related to `Customer_Service_Fact` (returns data source)

# Products

## Overview
A dimension table for products sold and purchased. Organises products into a three-level hierarchy: Category → Type → Product.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Product_ID | Business key for the product |
| Product Category No / Product Category | Top-level category (e.g. Computers) |
| Product Type No / Product Type | Mid-level type (e.g. Computers & Tablets) |
| Product No | Numeric product code |
| Product | Product name (e.g. Computer Accessories, Desktops, Laptops) |
| Product Category_Lang / Product Type_Lang / Product_Lang | Multi-language labels (null in sample data) |

## Sample Data
- All 5 sample products are in "Computers" → "Computers & Tablets" category/type.
- Products include: Computer Accessories, Desktops, Laptops, Netbooks, Tablets.

## Relationships
- `Product_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Product_ID`, `Customer_Service_Fact.Product_ID`, `CustomerLoyalty.Product_ID`, `Finance_Fact` (indirect), `Order_Fact.Product_ID`, `Pipeline_Fact.Product_ID`, `Procurement_Fact.Product_ID`

# Order_Fact

## Overview
The primary sales order fact table. Each row represents a sales transaction combining product, customer, sales rep, and channel with full revenue, cost, margin, discount, and pricing details.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| Sales_ID | FK to Geography |
| Channel_ID | FK to Channel |
| Planned / Revenue / Variance / Variance % | Revenue plan vs actuals |
| Gross Revenue | Pre-discount revenue |
| Costs / Margin / Margin % | Profitability |
| Discount / Discount % | Discount applied |
| Units Ordered / Units Shipped | Volume |
| Field18 / Field19 | Derived volume fields (÷5 of ordered/shipped) |
| List Price / Sale Price / Unit Cost | Pricing |
| Unit Cost | Cost per unit |

## Sample Data
- Product 28 (high value item, List Price ~$1,861) dominates first rows.
- Revenues range from $13,230 to $55,827.
- Margins can be negative (e.g. −$649, −$11,606) indicating loss-making orders.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Sales_ID` → `Geography.Sales_ID`
- `Channel_ID` → `Channel.Channel_ID`

# Time

## Overview
The primary date/time dimension table with daily granularity. Used as the core date dimension by most operational and sales fact tables. Provides full calendar hierarchy from day to year.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | Business key for the date |
| Year | Calendar year (e.g. "2014") |
| Quarter No / Quarter | Quarter number and label (e.g. "Q1/2014") |
| Month No / Month | Month number and label (e.g. "Jan/2014") |
| Week No / Week | ISO week number and label (e.g. "W1-2014") |
| Day of Week No / Day of Week | Day of week number and name |
| Day | Full date value |
| Day of Month No | Day number within the month |
| Day of Year No | Day number within the year |

## Sample Data
- Starts from 2014-01-01.
- Granularity is daily.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Date_ID`, `Customer_Service_Fact.Date_ID`, `CustomerLoyalty.Date_ID`, `IT_Fact.Day_ID`, `Order_Fact.Date_ID`, `Pipeline_Fact.Date_ID`, `Procurement_Fact.Date_ID`, `Recruiting_Fact.Date_ID`, `Training_Fact.Date_ID`
- Note: `HR_Calendar` and `Fiscal_Period` are separate calendar dimensions for HR and Finance subject areas respectively

# Sales_Stage

## Overview
A small lookup/dimension table for sales pipeline stages. Defines the 4 stages an opportunity progresses through before closing.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Stage_ID | Business key for the stage |
| Sales Stage | Stage label (Stage 1 through Stage 4) |

## Sample Data
| Stage_ID | Sales Stage |
|---|---|
| 1 | Stage 1 |
| 2 | Stage 2 |
| 3 | Stage 3 |
| 4 | Stage 4 |

## Relationships
- `Stage_ID` → referenced by `Pipeline_Fact.Stage_ID`

# Run Reports

## Overview
An audit/operational log table tracking individual report runs in the BI system. Each row records one report execution event with user, report path, timing, and status.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| User name | User who ran the report |
| Report name | Name of the report |
| Report type | Type (e.g. "Report batchreportservice") |
| Report search path | Full Cognos content store path |
| Execution time | Execution time in milliseconds |
| Month | Calendar month of execution |
| Time stamp | Exact timestamp of execution |
| Package | Folder path of the report |
| Status | SUCCESS or FAILURE |
| Error details | Error message if failed |

## Sample Data
- All sample rows are from user "Seninger, Glen" running "Can Opportunities Closed This Month".
- All executions were successful; execution times ~5,173–5,311 ms.
- Dates: Aug–Oct 2011.

## Relationships
- Standalone audit log; no foreign key relationships to other D2G tables
- Part of the BI usage audit suite alongside `Run Jobs`, `View Reports`, `User Logins`

# RevenueForecast

## Overview
A time-series forecast table for gross revenue and units ordered. Mirrors the structure of `ReturnsForecast` but for revenue and order volume. Used for revenue planning and predictive analytics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| Year / Month No | Calendar period of the actuals |
| Record_Count | Number of underlying transactions |
| Gross Revenue_Sum / Gross Revenue_Mean | Actual gross revenue (total and average) |
| Units Ordered_Sum / Units Ordered_Mean | Actual units ordered (total and average) |
| $TI_TimeIndex / $TI_TimeLabel / $TI_Year / $TI_Month | Time series index and label |
| $TI_Future | Flag: 0 = historical, 1 = future forecast |
| $TS-Gross Revenue_Sum / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + CI for Revenue Sum |
| $TS-Gross Revenue_Mean / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + CI for Revenue Mean |
| $TS-Units Ordered_Sum / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + CI for Units Ordered Sum |
| $TS-Units Ordered_Mean / $TSLCI / $TSUCI | Forecast + CI for Units Ordered Mean |

## Sample Data
- Actuals from 2014; time-series projections also mapped to 2014.
- Gross revenue sums $8M–$11M per month.
- Record counts 234–347 transactions per month.

## Relationships
- Standalone forecast table; not directly joined to other tables via foreign keys
- Logically related to `Order_Fact` and `CustomerLoyalty` (revenue data source)

# Training_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for employee training activity. Each row records a training enrollment event per employee, course, and date — tracking cost, duration, score, and program completion.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Course_ID | FK to Course |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Employee_ID | FK to Employee |
| Enrollment_Status | FK to Enrollment_Status (stored as numeric ID) |
| # Course | Number of courses taken |
| Course Duration | Duration of course (hours) |
| Course Score | Score achieved (0–100) |
| Training Hours | Total training hours |
| Training Cost | Cost of training |
| # Program | Number of programs |
| # Program Enrollment / # Program Completion | Program-level tracking |

## Sample Data
- Courses 1–5 across employees 989, 1162, 962, 1025, 524.
- Enrollment statuses 1–5 (Completed, Dev Complete, Dropped, Failed, In Progress) and 11.
- Costs range from $832 to $2,229 per enrollment.

## Relationships
- `Course_ID` → `Course.Course_ID`
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Employee_ID` → `Employee.Employee_ID`
- `Enrollment_Status` → `Enrollment_Status.Enrollment_ID`

# View Reports

## Overview
An audit log table tracking report view events in the BI system. Similar to `Run Reports` but captures interactive viewing (not batch execution), including encoded URL-based report paths.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| User name | Username viewing the report |
| Report name | Encoded or plain report name |
| Report type | Type of report object |
| Report format | Output format (NULL in samples) |
| Report search path | Cognos content store path or encoded URL ID |
| Month | Calendar month label |
| Time stamp | Timestamp of the view event |
| Error details | Error if any |
| Package | Parent folder path |
| Status | Success or Failure |

## Sample Data
- Rows from Oct/Aug/Sep 2011.
- Report names are Base64-encoded URLs in sample data.
- All statuses are "Success".

## Relationships
- Standalone audit log; no foreign key relationships to other D2G tables
- Part of the BI usage audit suite alongside `Run Jobs`, `Run Reports`, `User Logins`

# User Logins

## Overview
An audit log table tracking user login and session activity in the BI system. Each row represents one user session with logon/logoff timestamps and session duration.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| User name | Username |
| Month | Calendar month label |
| Logon time | Timestamp when session started |
| Session ID | Unique session identifier |
| Logoff operation | Type of logoff (e.g. "Logoff") |
| Logoff time | Timestamp when session ended |
| Session duration | Session length in seconds |

## Sample Data
- All sample rows from Nov/2011.
- Some sessions have 0 duration (instant logon/logoff or timeout).
- Williams, Wanda had a 173-second session.

## Relationships
- Standalone audit log; no foreign key relationships to other D2G tables
- Part of the BI usage audit suite alongside `Run Jobs`, `Run Reports`, `View Reports`

# Run Jobs

## Overview
An audit/operational log table tracking BI job (batch report) executions. Each row captures a job step within a job run, including execution time, status, and the Cognos content store path of the job.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| User name | User who ran the job |
| Job name and path | Full Cognos content store path of the job definition |
| Job status | Overall job status (Success/Failure) |
| Job error details | Error message if failed |
| Job execution time | Total job execution time (ms) |
| Job step name and path | Path of individual job step |
| Job step status | Status of the step |
| Job step error details | Step-level error message |
| Job step execution time | Step execution time (ms) |

## Sample Data
- All sample rows are from user "Bradford, Carol", job "CSA Enablements per Analyst".
- All steps succeeded; execution times range 11,938–25,656 ms.
- Same job run has multiple steps (one row per step).

## Relationships
- Standalone audit log; no foreign key relationships to other D2G tables
- Related to `Run Reports`, `View Reports`, and `User Logins` as part of the BI usage audit suite

# Workforce_Fact

## Overview
An extended HR fact table covering workforce planning metrics. Builds on `HR_Fact` with additional succession planning columns. Each row is one employee for one HR calendar period.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to HR_Calendar |
| Employee_ID | FK to Employee |
| Active Headcount | 1 if active |
| Headcount | Total headcount |
| Annual Salary / Avg Salary Amt | Compensation |
| # Involuntary Separation / # Voluntary Separation / # Separation | Separation counts |
| Separation % / Promotion % | Rates |
| # High Performers / # Steady Performers / # Low Performers | Performance buckets |
| High Performers % / Steady Performers % / Low Performers % | Performance distribution |
| # Successor | Number of identified successors |
| # Successor Required | Number of positions requiring a successor |
| # Successor Program | Succession programs in place |
| # Found Successor Positions | Positions with successors found |

## Sample Data
- Salaries range $81,040–$127,980.
- All sample rows have 0 successor metrics (succession planning not yet populated).
- Performance distribution: rows are flagged as High, Steady, or Low performers.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `HR_Calendar.Date_ID`
- `Employee_ID` → `Employee.Employee_ID`
- Superset of `HR_Fact` — adds succession planning columns

# Service_Type

## Overview
A lookup/dimension table for customer service call types. Defines the various categories of support requests a customer can raise.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Service_Type_ID | Business key for the service type |
| Service_Type | Type label (e.g. Billing Issue, Delivery Issue) |

## Sample Data (first 5 of more)
| Service_Type_ID | Service_Type |
|---|---|
| 1 | Billing Issue |
| 2 | Contact Information Change |
| 3 | Contract Cancellation Request |
| 4 | Delivery Issue |
| 5 | Functionality Question |

## Relationships
- `Service_Type_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Service_Type_ID`

# Vendor

## Overview
A dimension table for suppliers/vendors. Each row represents one vendor with geographic and classification details. Used in procurement and financial transactions.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Vendor_ID | Business key for the vendor |
| Vendor | Vendor company name |
| Vendor Type | Classification (all "Not Applicable" in sample data) |
| Country | Country of the vendor |
| City | City |
| State/Province | State or province |
| Vendor Address | Street address |

## Sample Data
- IDs 1–4 based in Australia (St. Kilda, Perth).
- IDs 5–10 based in Canada (Victoria, St. John's, Ottawa).
- All vendor types are "Not Applicable".

## Relationships
- `Vendor_ID` → referenced by `Finance_Fact.Vendor_ID`, `Procurement_Fact.Vendor_ID`

| Column | Description |

| Team Lead | Full name of the team lead |

## Sample Data

- All report to manager "Spencer Brett Franklin" under team lead "Lester Leon Barrett".

## Relationships
- `Agent_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Agent_ID` (fact table for call center metrics)

# Attrition_Study

## Overview
A denormalized analytical table (blended fact/dimension) capturing employee attrition, performance, training, and fleet metrics in a single wide row per employee event. Used for workforce analytics and predictive attrition modelling.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| Employee_ID | Foreign key to Employee |
| Active Headcount | 1 if currently active, 0 if separated |
| # Separation | Number of separations |
| # Involuntary Separation | Count of involuntary separations |
| # Voluntary Separation | Count of voluntary separations |
| Performance | Performance score |
| Tenure | Tenure in months |
| Age at Metric Date | Age in months at measurement date |
| Annual Salary | Annual compensation |
| Job Family / Job / Position | Role classification |
| Geography / Country / Location / Town or City | Location details |
| Gender / Ethnic Group | Demographic attributes |
| Organization / Organization Class / Organization Type | Org hierarchy |
| Training Hours/Cost/Score/Duration/# Course | Training metrics (overall + by type: Technical, Leadership, Sales) |
| Age of Primary Truck | Age of assigned fleet vehicle |
| Miles Driven TTM (k) | Miles driven in trailing twelve months (thousands) |
| $E-# Separation | Predicted probability of separation |

## Sample Data
- Rows cover employees across Europe and North America in Production jobs.
- Many employees have zero training (0 hours, 0 cost).

## Relationships
- `Employee_ID` → `Employee.Employee_ID`
- Overlaps dimensionally with `HR_Fact` and `Workforce_Fact` but is a standalone analytical extract

# Applicant

A dimension table for job applicants used in the recruiting process. Classifies applicants by type (Internal/External) and employment basis (Full Time, Part Time, Student).

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Applicant_ID | Business key for the applicant |
| Applicant | Applicant identifier code (e.g. ID1001) |
| Applicant Type | Internal or External |
| Full Part Time | Employment type: Full Time, Part Time, or Student |

## Sample Data
- Mix of Internal/External applicants with Full Time, Part Time, and Student designations.
- Applicant codes follow the pattern ID1001, ID1002, etc.

## Relationships
- `Applicant_ID` → referenced as `Application_ID` in `Recruiting_Fact` (recruiting metrics fact table)

# Applicant

## Overview
A dimension table for job applicants used in the recruiting process. Classifies applicants by type (Internal/External) and employment basis (Full Time, Part Time, Student).

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Applicant_ID | Business key for the applicant |
| Applicant | Applicant identifier code (e.g. ID1001) |
| Applicant Type | Internal or External |
| Full Part Time | Employment type: Full Time, Part Time, or Student |

## Sample Data
- Mix of Internal/External applicants with Full Time, Part Time, and Student designations.

arguments.content:
# Call_Center_Fact

## Overview
The primary fact table for call center operations. Each row represents one call interaction event, linking agents, customers, products, call severity, and service type with detailed call performance metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Agent_ID | FK to Agents |
| Date_ID | FK to Time (date dimension) |
| Group_ID | FK to Group (agent group/level) |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Call_Severity_ID | FK to Call_Severity |
| Service_Type_ID | FK to Service_Type |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| # Call | Number of calls |
| # Completed | Completed calls count |
| # Active Agent | Active agents at time of call |
| # Terminated | Terminated calls |
| Avg Hold Time | Average hold time (minutes) |
| Call Duration | Total call duration (minutes) |
| Call Costs | Cost of calls |
| Hold Time Mising SLA / Hold Time with SLA | SLA compliance for hold time |
| Call Duration missing SLA / Call Duration within SLA | SLA compliance for duration |
| Quality Level | Quality score (0–1) |
| Satisfaction Level | Customer satisfaction score (0–1) |
| Tech Level | Technical resolution score (0–1) |

## Sample Data
- Calls span various agents, products, groups, and service types.
- Quality and satisfaction levels typically range 0.7–1.0.

## Relationships
- `Agent_ID` → `Agents.Agent_ID`
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Group_ID` → `Group.Group_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Call_Severity_ID` → `Call_Severity.Call_Severity_ID`
- `Service_Type_ID` → `Service_Type.Service_Type_ID`
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`

# Customer

## Overview
A dimension table for customers. Each row represents a customer account with classification, geographic, credit, and sales representative details.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Customer_ID | Business key for the customer |
| Customer | Customer name |
| Customer Type No / Customer Type | Type classification (e.g. Retail, Internet Direct, Branded Stores, Entertainment Venues) |
| Customer Code | Numeric customer code |
| Credit Rating | High, Medium, or Low |
| Active | Y/N flag |
| Country / State / City / Address | Geographic location |
| Status | Active or Inactive |
| Sales Rep ID / Sales Rep | Assigned sales representative |
| Receivables ID / Receivables Analyst | Accounts receivable contact |

## Sample Data
- Customers span Germany, Italy, Ireland, Poland — international scope.
- Types include Entertainment Venues, Retail, Internet Direct, Branded Stores.

## Relationships
- `Customer_ID` → referenced by `Call_Center_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Customer_Service_Fact.Customer_ID`, `CustomerLoyalty.Customer_ID`, `Finance_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Order_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Pipeline_Fact.Customer_ID`, `Procurement_Fact` (indirect)

arguments.content:
# Customer_Service_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for customer service orders/transactions. Tracks order revenue, returns, late products, and customer gain/loss metrics over time per product and customer.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| Sales_ID | FK to Geography (sales rep territory) |
| Revenue | Actual revenue |
| Return Revenue | Revenue lost to returns |
| # Lost Customer / # New Customer | Customer churn and acquisition count |
| Lost Customer % / New Customer % | Churn/acquisition rates |
| # Late Product / Late Product Days | Late delivery metrics |
| Return to Order % | Return rate vs orders |
| Units Ordered / Units Returned | Volume metrics |
| Order Amt | Order amount |

## Sample Data
- Revenue ranges from $56 to $1,008 per row.
- Units ordered 4–9 with small fractional returns.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Sales_ID` → `Geography.Sales_ID`

arguments.content:
# Finance_Fact

## Overview
The primary financial fact table. Each row captures a financial period snapshot per customer, organization, and vendor — including P&L, balance sheet ratios, payables/receivables, and cash flow metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| Date_ID | FK to Fiscal_Period |
| Org_ID | FK to Organization |
| Vendor_ID | FK to Vendor |
| Total Revenue | Total revenue |
| Cost of Goods Sold | COGS |
| Gross Profit | Revenue minus COGS |
| Operating Expense | OpEx |
| Net Income | Gross Profit minus OpEx |
| Budget / Variance | Budget vs actual |
| Current Assets / Current Liabilities | Balance sheet items |
| Opening Inventory / Closing Inventory | Inventory values |
| Inventory Turnover / Current Ratio / Quick Ratio | Liquidity ratios |
| Invoice Amt / Payment Amt / Late Payment | AR/AP metrics |
| DSO / DPO | Days Sales Outstanding / Days Payable Outstanding |
| # Invoice / # Open Invoice / # Payment | Transaction counts |
| Cash Inflow / Cash Outflow | Cash flow |
| Discounts Offered / Discount Taken / Discount Refused | Discount tracking |

## Sample Data
- Net income ranges from negative (−$18,183) to positive ($35,669) per period.
- DSO ~51–72 days, DPO ~49–68 days.

## Relationships
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Date_ID` → `Fiscal_Period.Date_ID`
- `Org_ID` → `Organization.Org_ID`

# Finance_Fact

## Overview
The primary financial fact table. Each row captures a financial period snapshot per customer, organization, and vendor — including P&L, balance sheet ratios, payables/receivables, and cash flow metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Customer_ID | FK to Customer |
| Date_ID | FK to Fiscal_Period |
| Org_ID | FK to Organization |
| Vendor_ID | FK to Vendor |
| Total Revenue | Total revenue |
| Cost of Goods Sold | COGS |
| Gross Profit | Revenue minus COGS |
| Operating Expense | OpEx |
| Net Income | Gross Profit minus OpEx |
| Budget / Variance | Budget vs actual |
| Current Assets / Current Liabilities | Balance sheet items |
| Opening Inventory / Closing Inventory | Inventory values |
| Inventory Turnover / Current Ratio / Quick Ratio | Liquidity ratios |
| Invoice Amt / Payment Amt / Late Payment | AR/AP metrics |
| DSO / DPO | Days Sales Outstanding / Days Payable Outstanding |
| # Invoice / # Open Invoice / # Payment | Transaction counts |
| Cash Inflow / Cash Outflow | Cash flow |
| Discounts Offered / Discount Taken / Discount Refused | Discount tracking |

## Sample Data
- Net income ranges from negative (−$18,183) to positive ($35,669) per period.
- DSO ~51–72 days, DPO ~49–68 days.

## Relationships
- `Customer_ID` → `Customer.Customer_ID`
- `Date_ID` → `Fiscal_Period.Date_ID`
- `Org_ID` → `Organization.Org_ID`
- `Vendor_ID` → `Vendor.Vendor_ID`

arguments.content:
# Fiscal_Period

## Overview
A fiscal calendar dimension table. Maps `Date_ID` values to fiscal year, quarter, and month labels (using "FY" prefix). Used specifically by financial fact tables.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | Business key for the fiscal period |
| Year | Fiscal year label (e.g. "FY09") |
| Quarter No / Quarter | Quarter number and label (e.g. "Q1/FY09") |
| Month No / Month | Month number and label (e.g. "P01/FY09") |

## Sample Data
- Fiscal year starts at FY09 (2009).
- Months labelled as P01 through P12 within each fiscal year.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → referenced by `Finance_Fact.Date_ID`
- Note: Different from `Time` and `HR_Calendar` — this is the **fiscal** calendar used for finance reporting

arguments.content:
# Employee

## Overview
A dimension table for employees. Each row represents one employee with full demographic, role, organizational, and geographic profile.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Employee_ID | Business key for the employee |
| Employee / Employee Name | Employee name |
| Employee ID | Numeric employee ID |
| Age | Age in years (decimal) |
| Date of Birth | DOB |
| Gender | Male/Female |
| Ethnic Group | Ethnicity |
| Marital Status | Marital status (may be null) |
| Disabled Flag | Disability flag (may be null) |
| Job Family / Job / Position | Role classification |
| Original Hire Date | Date first hired |
| Geography / Country / Location / Town or City | Location details |
| Organization / Organization Class / Organization Type | Org hierarchy |
| Tenure | Tenure band (e.g. "(5) 10+", "(4) 5 to <10") |

## Sample Data
- Employees span France, UK, Canada, USA.
- Job families include Production and Operations.
- Tenure bands range from 5–10+ years.

## Relationships
- `Employee_ID` → referenced by `HR_Fact.Employee_ID`, `Workforce_Fact.Employee_ID`, `Attrition_Study.Employee_ID`, `Recruiting_Fact.Employee_ID`, `Training_Fact.Employee_ID`
- `Employee ID` (numeric) → referenced by `Geography.Employee ID`

arguments.content:
# HR_Calendar

## Overview
A calendar dimension for HR reporting. Maps `Date_ID` values to calendar year, quarter, and month using standard calendar year (not fiscal). Used specifically by HR fact tables.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | Business key for the period |
| Year | Calendar year (e.g. 2009) |
| Quarter No / Quarter | Quarter number and label (e.g. "Q1/2009") |
| Month No / Month | Month number and name (e.g. "January") |

## Sample Data
- Starts from January 2009.
- Month names are spelled out (January, February...) unlike `Fiscal_Period` which uses "P01/FY09".

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → referenced by `HR_Fact.Date_ID` and `Workforce_Fact.Date_ID`
- Parallel to `Fiscal_Period` (finance) and `Time` (daily operations) — each serves a different subject area

+| Quarter No / Quarter | Quarter number and label (e.g. "Q1/2009") |
+| Month No / Month | Month number and name (e.g. "January") |
+
+## Sample Data
+- Starts from January 2009.
+- Month names are spelled out (January, February...) unlike `Fiscal_Period` which uses "P01/FY09".
+
+## Relationships
+- `Date_ID` → referenced by `HR_Fact.Date_ID` and `Workforce_Fact.Date_ID`
+- Parallel to `Fiscal_Period` (finance) and `Time` (daily operations) — each serves a different subject area

arguments.content:
# HR_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for core HR metrics per employee per period. Tracks headcount, salary, separations, promotions, and performance distribution over time.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Date_ID | FK to HR_Calendar |
| Employee_ID | FK to Employee |
| Active Headcount | 1 if active, 0 if not |
| Headcount | Total headcount count |
| Annual Salary / Avg Salary Amt | Compensation metrics |
| # Involuntary Separation / # Voluntary Separation / # Separation | Separation counts |
| Separation % | Separation rate |
| # Promotion / Promotion % | Promotion metrics |
| # High Performers / # Steady Performers / # Low Performers | Performance buckets |
| High Performers % / Steady Performers % / Low Performers % | Performance distribution ratios |

## Sample Data
- Each row is one employee for one period.
- Salaries range from $41,300 to $142,789.
- Performance is categorised as High, Steady, or Low — one flag set per row.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `HR_Calendar.Date_ID`
- `Employee_ID` → `Employee.Employee_ID`

# IT_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for IT/BI system usage metrics. Tracks user sessions, report executions, server performance, and application usage across the BI infrastructure.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Day_ID | FK to Time (daily grain) |
| Period | Peak or off-peak period |
| Server | Server name (e.g. COGVMLN4) |
| ServerFarm | Server farm type (e.g. AIX) |
| ApplicationType | Type of application (e.g. BI) |
| ServerLocation | Physical location of the server |
| Application | Application name (e.g. "Procurement Analytics") |
| Status | Execution status (Success/Failure) |
| NoofRequests | Number of requests in the session |
| User | Username |
| NoofUsers | Number of users in the session |
| ExecutionTime (Secs) | Execution time in seconds |
| Session duration (Mins) | Session length in minutes |
| CPUUsage | CPU utilisation |
| Failures | Number of failures |

## Sample Data
- All sample rows are "Success" status, off-peak period, AIX server farm.
- Applications include Procurement Analytics, Supply Chain, Financial Reporting, etc.
- CPU usage is low (~0.08–0.18).

## Relationships
- `Day_ID` → `Time.Date_ID` (daily calendar grain)

arguments.content:
# Procurement_Fact

## Overview
A fact table for procurement/purchasing operations. Tracks purchase orders per buyer, vendor, product, and organisation with compliance, rejection, and delivery performance metrics.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Buyer_ID | ID of the buyer (employee making the purchase) |
| Date_ID | FK to Time |
| Org_ID | FK to Organization |
| Vendor_ID | FK to Vendor |
| Product_ID | FK to Products |
| Compliance Amt | Amount purchased within contract |
| Purchase Amt | Total purchase amount |
| Purchase Amt Outside Contract | Amount purchased outside contract |
| Potential Saving | Potential cost saving |
| Unit Price | Price per unit |
| Units Purchased / Units Rejected / Units Received | Volume and quality metrics |
| Units Purchased Outside Contract | Non-compliant volume |
| # Order / # Reject | Order and rejection counts |
| # Late Delivery / Late Delivery Days | Delivery performance |
| Rejected to Received % | Rejection rate |
| Received to Ordered % | Fulfilment rate |

## Sample Data
- Fulfilment rates ~80–97%.
- Rejection rates up to 23%.
- Some orders have zero late deliveries.

## Relationships
- `Date_ID` → `Time.Date_ID`
- `Org_ID` → `Organization.Org_ID`
- `Vendor_ID` → `Vendor.Vendor_ID`
- `Product_ID` → `Products.Product_ID`
- `Buyer_ID` → likely maps to `Employee.Employee_ID`

# Organization

## Overview
A dimension table defining the corporate organizational hierarchy. Maps `Org_ID` to three levels of the organisation tree.

## Columns
| Column | Description |
|---|---|
| ID | Surrogate primary key |
| Org_ID | Business key for the organisation unit |
| Organization_Level1 | Top-level (e.g. Corporate) |
| Organization_Level2 | Second level (e.g. Sales and Service, Marketing) |
| Organization_Level3 | Third level (e.g. Worldwide Sales, Services, Business Development) |

## Sample Data
- All rows roll up to "Corporate" at Level 1.
- Level 2 includes "Sales and Service" and "Marketing".
- Level 3 includes Worldwide Sales, Services, Business Development, Demand Generation, Events.

## Relationships
- `Org_ID` → referenced by `Finance_Fact.Org_ID`, `Procurement_Fact.Org_ID`
